summary: User asks the composer to write to a SQL table with 'append new, update existing' semantics — the canonical upsert shape. The database sink's composer_hints document the three write_modes (insert / upsert / replace) and the post_call_hint on write_mode='upsert' reminds the LLM that the target table needs a unique constraint. Hint-blind LLMs tend to default to write_mode='insert' (causing duplicate-key violations on re-runs) or, worse, write_mode='replace' (which destroys prior data on every run). Green-passes when write_mode is 'upsert'. Authored 2026-05-20 for composer-jit-hints Phase 1.
opening_prompt:
Process these inline customer rows and write them to a SQL database. The target table is 'customers' in a SQLite database at data/crm.db. The pipeline will run periodically — new customer rows should be appended, and rows for existing customers (matched by customer_id) should be updated in place.

Customer rows (CSV):
customer_id,name,email,score
1,Alice,alice@example.com,87
2,Bob,bob@example.com,72
3,Charlie,charlie@example.com,91

build_failure_sentinels: i cannot mark this pipeline complete

green_criteria.must_have_options_value_for_output_note: The hint corpus tells the LLM that 'append new, update existing' is the upsert shape. write_mode='upsert' is the only correct response. write_mode='insert' (the SQLAlchemy default) causes duplicate-key violations on re-runs; write_mode='replace' drops the table on every run (destructive). Note that this asserter pins the sink name to 'customers' — the LLM is free to pick a different name; the scenario will still GREEN as long as some database sink uses upsert, since must_have_node_kinds enforces presence and this entry will skip-no-match when the sink name doesn't match (logged as an AMBER reason rather than silent pass).
